learning_objectives: Understand the role of audio in player immersion and gameplay feedback
Identify the components of a game audio pipeline
Differentiate between linear and interactive audio
readings: A Composer's Guide to Game Music Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Game Audio and Sound Design. Audio is often called the invisible half of the gaming experience — it shapes emotion, reinforces mechanics, and grounds players in virtual worlds.
key_concepts: Properties of sound: frequency, amplitude, timbre, and envelope (ADSR)
Digital audio fundamentals: sample rate, bit depth, and codec formats
The game audio pipeline from asset creation to runtime playback
Differences between linear media audio and interactive game audio
Overview of professional game audio tools and DAWs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparison of gameplay clips with and without audio; walkthrough of a DAW (Reaper) and its basic signal flow
setup_requirements: Studio headphones or quality monitoring speakers

review.value: Attack, Decay, Sustain, Release — the four phases that shape a sound's amplitude over time
Number of audio samples captured per second; 44100 Hz (CD quality) or 48000 Hz (professional standard for games)

class_schedule.topic: Sound Effect Creation: Foley and Synthesis
learning_objectives: Record and edit Foley sound effects for common game actions
Design sound effects using subtractive and FM synthesis
Apply layering and processing techniques to create polished game sounds
readings: Designing Sound by Andy Farnell Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Sound effects are the tactile feedback of games. Today we explore two core creation methods: recording real-world sounds (Foley) and generating them electronically (synthesis).
key_concepts: Foley recording technique: microphone selection, room treatment, and performance
Common Foley categories in games: footsteps, impacts, cloth, UI feedback
Subtractive synthesis: oscillators, filters, and envelopes for SFX
FM synthesis for metallic, bell-like, and sci-fi sound design
Layering multiple sources and processing with EQ, compression, and reverb
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live Foley recording session for footstep and weapon impact sounds; synthesizing a laser blast in Vital synthesizer
setup_requirements: Portable audio recorder or USB microphone
Reaper project template from course resources

review.value: The art of recording real-world sounds to replace or enhance audio in post-production, named after sound artist Jack Foley
Starting with a harmonically rich waveform and sculpting it by removing frequencies with filters

learning_objectives: Design music that responds dynamically to gameplay state changes
Implement horizontal and vertical re-sequencing techniques
Build a layered adaptive music system in FMOD Studio
readings: A Composer's Guide to Game Music Ch. 7-9
FMOD Studio documentation: Music system overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Unlike film scores that play linearly, game music must react to unpredictable player actions. Adaptive music systems allow seamless musical transitions driven by gameplay parameters.
key_concepts: Horizontal re-sequencing: branching between musical segments at transition points
Vertical remixing: adding or removing instrument layers based on game state
Stinger systems for one-shot musical events tied to gameplay moments
Tempo and key synchronization across adaptive segments
FMOD music timeline and parameter-driven transitions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a three-intensity combat music system in FMOD Studio with smooth crossfade transitions
setup_requirements: Provided music stems (exploration, tension, combat layers)

review.value: Switching between different musical sections or branches at defined transition points based on game events
Layering or muting individual instrument tracks within a single musical piece to change intensity

learning_objectives: Implement 3D spatialization for sound sources in a game engine
Configure attenuation curves and occlusion for realistic sound propagation
Apply HRTF-based binaural rendering for headphone playback

lecture_notes.introduction: Spatial audio transforms flat stereo mixes into immersive soundscapes. Accurate sound positioning gives players critical gameplay information and deepens the sense of presence.
key_concepts: Sound attenuation models: linear, logarithmic, and custom rolloff curves
Occlusion and obstruction: simulating sound blocked by geometry
HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function) for binaural 3D positioning
Reverb zones and environmental acoustics simulation
Ambisonics and channel-based surround sound formats
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring a Unity scene with FMOD 3D spatializer, attenuation curves, and occlusion raycast system
setup_requirements: Provided 3D test scene with multiple sound emitters

review.value: The decrease in sound volume as distance increases between the listener and the source, modeled by rolloff curves
Head-Related Transfer Function — models how human ears perceive sound direction through frequency filtering and inter-aural time/level differences

class_schedule.topic: Audio Middleware: FMOD and Wwise in Production
learning_objectives: Architect a complete game audio project using FMOD or Wwise
Implement parameter-driven sound events with randomization and variation
Profile audio CPU and memory usage and apply optimization strategies
readings: FMOD Studio User Manual: Events and Parameters

lecture_notes.introduction: Audio middleware bridges the gap between sound designers and programmers. FMOD and Wwise are the industry standard tools that allow complex, data-driven audio behaviors without recompiling game code.
key_concepts: Event-based audio architecture: decoupling sound design from code
Game parameters driving real-time audio changes (RPM, health, distance)
Randomization containers for variation in repeated sounds
Sound banks: packaging, streaming, and memory management
Profiling tools for CPU usage, voice count, and memory budgets
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a vehicle engine audio system in FMOD with RPM and load parameters driving pitch, crossfade, and exhaust pops
setup_requirements: Provided vehicle audio assets (engine layers, exhaust samples)
Unity profiler for audio performance monitoring

review.value: A self-contained sound behavior definition in middleware that can be triggered by game code without knowing its internal structure
A compiled binary package of audio assets and event metadata loaded at runtime, optimized for memory and streaming

class_schedule.topic: Voice Acting Direction and Dialogue Systems
learning_objectives: Direct voice acting sessions that produce consistent, high-quality performances
Design and implement a branching dialogue audio system
Apply mixing and mastering techniques to finalize game audio assets
readings: A Composer's Guide to Game Music Ch. 12
Dialogue Systems in Games (GDC Vault lecture notes)

lecture_notes.introduction: Voice acting brings characters to life, and dialogue systems must deliver those performances seamlessly during gameplay. Today we also cover the final stage of the audio pipeline: mixing and mastering the complete soundscape.
key_concepts: Casting and audition processes for game voice talent
Directing techniques: reference delivery, emotional range, and consistency
Dialogue management: triggering, queuing, priority, and interruption rules
Procedural audio generation for dynamic, non-repetitive ambient sound
Final mix: balancing music, SFX, dialogue, and ambience with bus routing and sidechain ducking
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mock voice direction session with provided script; implementing a dialogue priority system in FMOD with ducking
setup_requirements: FMOD Studio project from previous sessions
Provided dialogue script and placeholder voice recordings

review.value: Automatically lowering music and ambient audio levels when dialogue plays to ensure speech intelligibility
Sound generated algorithmically at runtime rather than from recorded samples, enabling infinite variation and reduced memory usage

quizzes.topic: Audio Fundamentals and Sound Effect Design

questions.question: A game requires footstep sounds for a character walking on gravel. Describe a Foley recording approach and explain how you would process the raw recordings to make them game-ready, including at least three specific processing steps.
questions.answer: Record multiple footstep performances on real gravel using a close-miked shotgun or cardioid microphone in a quiet environment. Processing steps: (1) trim and normalize each step to consistent peak levels, (2) apply high-pass filtering around 80-100 Hz to remove low-frequency rumble and room noise, (3) add subtle EQ boost in the 2-5 kHz range to enhance the crunch and detail of the gravel, (4) apply light compression to tame transient peaks while preserving dynamics, and (5) export as individual one-shot samples at 48 kHz / 16-bit for middleware integration.

rubric.key: Describes appropriate Foley recording technique
Lists at least three specific processing steps with reasoning
Mentions game-ready export considerations (format, sample rate)

questions.question: Explain the difference between horizontal re-sequencing and vertical remixing in adaptive music systems. Give a concrete gameplay example where each technique would be most appropriate.
questions.answer: Horizontal re-sequencing switches between different musical sections at defined transition points — for example, transitioning from an exploration melody to a combat theme when enemies are detected, with the switch occurring at the next bar boundary. Vertical remixing adds or removes instrument layers within the same musical piece — for example, gradually adding percussion, bass, and brass layers as the player progresses deeper into a dungeon, increasing tension without changing the harmonic structure. Horizontal is best for distinct state changes (peaceful to combat), while vertical is best for gradual intensity shifts within a single state.

rubric.key: Correctly defines horizontal re-sequencing
Correctly defines vertical remixing
Provides appropriate gameplay examples for each

questions.question: A player is standing in a stone corridor. An explosion occurs in a room behind a closed wooden door. Describe how an audio engine should process this sound differently than if the door were open, referencing specific spatial audio concepts.
questions.answer: With the door closed, the audio engine should apply occlusion filtering: a low-pass filter attenuates high frequencies (above approximately 1-2 kHz) because the wooden door absorbs and blocks higher-frequency sound waves while allowing lower frequencies to pass through. The direct path volume should be reduced based on the door material's transmission loss value. Additionally, the engine should route some signal through a muffled reverb to simulate sound traveling through the door material rather than open air. When the door is open, the full-spectrum direct sound reaches the player with normal distance attenuation, and the reverb characteristics of both the corridor and the source room blend naturally. The engine should also account for diffraction around the door frame edges in the open case.

rubric.key: Correctly applies occlusion concept with frequency filtering
Explains material-based transmission loss
Contrasts open vs closed door audio path differences

questions.question: In FMOD Studio, you are designing a vehicle engine sound system. The game exposes two parameters: engine RPM (0-8000) and throttle load (0.0-1.0). Describe how you would structure the FMOD event to produce a realistic, continuously varying engine sound.
questions.answer: Create a multi-instrument event with RPM mapped to a continuous game parameter on the timeline or a parameter sheet. Record or source engine samples at multiple RPM points (e.g., idle at 800, 2000, 4000, 6000, 8000 RPM) for both on-throttle and off-throttle states. Place these as loop regions in an FMOD scatterer instrument or multi-instrument, using RPM as the parameter axis with crossfade regions between adjacent samples so blending is smooth. Map the throttle load parameter to control crossfade between the on-throttle (aggressive, loaded) and off-throttle (deceleration, coasting) sample sets. Add a pitch modulator linked to RPM for fine-grained pitch interpolation between sample points. Layer in an exhaust pop one-shot triggered randomly during rapid throttle-off transitions using a conditional probability setting. Use a parameter-driven send to a low-pass filter for interior vs exterior cab simulation.

rubric.key: Describes multi-sample RPM crossfade structure
Explains throttle load parameter usage for tonal variation
Mentions pitch modulation and transition handling
Demonstrates understanding of FMOD event architecture

major_assignments.title: Complete Sound Design Package for a Game Level
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a complete audio package for a provided game level prototype. This includes environmental ambience, character sound effects, interactive object sounds, UI audio feedback, and an adaptive music system that responds to at least two gameplay parameters.
objectives: Demonstrate proficiency in Foley recording and synthesis for game SFX
Build an adaptive music system with horizontal and vertical techniques
Implement spatial audio with proper attenuation, occlusion, and reverb zones
Integrate all audio through FMOD Studio with proper event architecture
deliverables: FMOD Studio project with all events, parameters, and banks
Unity project with integrated audio playing correctly in-game
Audio asset library with source files and processed game-ready versions
Technical document describing the audio architecture and parameter mappings (3-4 pages)

criteria: Original sound effects are well-crafted and appropriate for the game context
Variety and randomization prevent repetition fatigue
Audio assets are technically clean (no clicks, pops, or unwanted noise)
Consistent loudness and tonal quality across the asset library
Music transitions smoothly between states without audible glitches
At least two gameplay parameters drive musical changes
Musical content is compositionally coherent across all states
Transition timing feels natural and responsive
3D sounds are properly spatialized with appropriate attenuation
Occlusion and obstruction are correctly applied
Reverb zones match the acoustic properties of the environment
Listener and emitter positions update correctly during gameplay
Clear description of audio architecture decisions
Parameter mapping tables for all interactive audio
Asset list with source descriptions and processing chains
Professional formatting and technical writing
Comprehensive implementation of all audio systems
Exceptional sound design quality and creativity
Seamless integration with gameplay through middleware
Professional-level documentation and presentation

midterm_exam.format: Mixed format: listening analysis, technical questions, and practical sound design exercise

questions.question: You are the audio lead on an open-world RPG. Describe your complete audio architecture plan, including how you would handle ambient soundscapes across diverse biomes, adaptive music for exploration and combat, and dialogue systems for hundreds of NPCs. Address memory and CPU budget constraints for a console platform with a 64 MB audio memory limit.
questions.expected_answer: The architecture should use a zone-based ambient system with crossfading between biome audio profiles, each containing layered loops and randomized one-shot emitters. Adaptive music uses a state machine with horizontal re-sequencing for major state changes (exploration, stealth, combat) and vertical remixing for intensity within each state. NPC dialogue uses a priority and queue system with automatic ducking, streaming from disk rather than loading into memory. Memory management uses sound banks organized by area/level for on-demand loading and unloading, with a persistent bank for global UI and player sounds. CPU optimization includes voice limiting with priority and virtualization, distance-based LOD for spatial processing, and pooled reverb sends rather than per-source convolution.

rubric.key: Describes ambient zone system with biome handling
Explains adaptive music architecture with state transitions
Addresses dialogue system with priority management
Provides concrete memory and CPU optimization strategies

questions.question: Compare and contrast FMOD Studio and Audiokinetic Wwise as game audio middleware solutions. Discuss their strengths, workflow differences, and scenarios where one might be preferred over the other.
questions.expected_answer: FMOD Studio uses a timeline-based event editor that feels intuitive for audio professionals coming from DAW backgrounds, with a straightforward parameter system and built-in mixing. Wwise uses an actor-mixer hierarchy and a more complex but powerful state/switch system suited for large-scale productions. FMOD is often preferred for indie and mid-sized teams due to its lower learning curve and free licensing for small projects. Wwise excels in AAA productions with its advanced profiling tools, SoundSeed procedural audio, and interactive music system (Wwise Interactive Music Hierarchy). Both support major engines (Unity, Unreal). Wwise has stronger spatial audio with its built-in Reflect and Rooms system, while FMOD relies more on third-party or engine-level spatial solutions.

rubric.key: Accurately describes FMOD strengths and workflow
Accurately describes Wwise strengths and workflow
Identifies appropriate use-case scenarios for each
Technical accuracy of comparison

exercises.title: Sound Effect Design Challenge
exercises.description: Design three game sound effects from scratch using only provided raw recordings and a synthesizer: a magical spell cast, a heavy door opening, and a UI confirmation chime.
requirements: Each sound must use at least two layers
Appropriate processing chain for each sound
Brief written description of design approach for each

exercises.title: FMOD Event Construction
exercises.description: Build an FMOD Studio event for a weather system that transitions between clear, rain, and thunderstorm states driven by a single intensity parameter (0.0 to 1.0).
requirements: Randomized thunder timing at high intensity
Spatialized rain drop impacts at medium-high intensity
Parameter-driven wind howl pitch and volume

labs_and_projects.title: Foley Recording and Processing Lab
objectives: Record high-quality Foley sounds for common game actions
Apply professional processing chains to raw recordings
Build a reusable game audio asset library
pre_lab_preparation: Review microphone polar patterns and placement techniques
Gather props for Foley performance (shoes, fabric, metal objects)
Install and configure Reaper DAW with provided FX plugins
materials_needed: Portable recorder or USB condenser microphone
Quiet recording space (studio or treated room)

instructions.title: Recording Session Setup
steps: Configure microphone input levels with test recordings
Set recording format to 48 kHz / 24-bit WAV
Record slate takes identifying each sound
instructions.expected_output: Properly configured recording session with clean signal chain

instructions.title: Foley Performance and Capture
steps: Record 10+ footstep variations on three surface types
Capture weapon or tool interaction sounds (sword draw, item pickup)
Record environmental interaction sounds (door, chest, lever)
Perform multiple takes for each sound with varying intensity
instructions.expected_output: Library of raw Foley recordings organized by category

instructions.title: Processing and Export
steps: Trim, normalize, and remove noise from recordings
Apply EQ, compression, and spatial processing
Create variations through pitch shifting and time stretching
Export game-ready assets with consistent naming convention
instructions.expected_output: Processed game-ready sound effect library with metadata

assessment_criteria.description: Clean recordings with appropriate signal levels, minimal noise floor, and correct technical specifications
Appropriate EQ, dynamics processing, and effects applied with clear artistic intent
Consistent naming, proper metadata, and logical folder structure for game integration
Sounds are evocative, appropriate for game use, and demonstrate artistic sensibility

submission_requirements: Complete Reaper project file with all sessions
Processed asset library in organized folder structure
Recording log documenting each take and processing chain
Brief reflection on recording challenges and solutions
bonus_challenges: Create a synthesized version of each Foley sound for comparison
Design a hybrid sound combining Foley and synthesis layers
Record and process ambience for a specific game environment

project.title: Original Game Audio Demo Reel
project.overview: Students will create a professional demo reel demonstrating mastery of game audio by redesigning the complete audio for a provided game prototype or a short game of their own creation. The reel must showcase sound design, adaptive music, spatial audio, and middleware integration.

minimum_requirements: Complete sound effect replacement for all gameplay actions
Original adaptive music system with at least three states
Spatial audio implementation with occlusion and reverb
Dialogue or narration system with mixing integration
FMOD or Wwise project with professional event architecture
Video demo reel with before/after comparison
requirements: All audio must be original (recorded, synthesized, or composed)
Adaptive music must respond to at least two gameplay parameters
Spatial audio must use 3D positioning with attenuation and occlusion
Professional mixing and mastering across all audio categories
Demo reel video must be 3-5 minutes showcasing all audio systems
Written breakdown of audio design decisions and implementation

project_phases.title: Integration and Mixing

project_phases.title: Demo Reel and Presentation

Portfolio-ready project documentation

Creative use of Foley, synthesis, and processing techniques
Variety and randomization preventing repetition

Technical quality (no artifacts, appropriate loudness)

submission_requirements: Final game build with integrated audio
FMOD/Wwise project with all source assets
Audio design document and technical breakdown

bonus_opportunities: Implement procedural audio generation for environmental sounds
Create an interactive audio installation or experience

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions, recording sessions, and project presentations. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in course failure.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Individual assignments must be completed independently. Audio assets must be original work — no royalty-free library sounds in graded submissions unless explicitly permitted.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All audio content must be original or properly attributed. Use of AI-generated audio must be disclosed and approved by the instructor. Plagiarism or uncredited sample use will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have access to a DAW (Reaper recommended), FMOD Studio, studio headphones, and a recording device. Lab studio equipment is available during scheduled hours.
